```markdown
---
title: "Premier League: The Growing Divide"
subtitle: "33 seasons of English football's increasing stratification"
author: "Eric Schnitger"
date: 2026-04-27
categories: [data-visualization, python, sports-analytics, web-scraping]
image: thumbnail.png
description: |
 An interactive analysis of every Premier League season from 1992 to 2025,
 examining title-winning points, Champions League qualification, relegation
 thresholds, and the growing gap between top and bottom.
format:
 html:
   code-fold: true
   code-summary: "Show code"
   code-tools: true
   toc: true
   toc-depth: 3
execute:
 echo: true
 warning: false
 freeze: auto
---

## The Question

In 1992/93, Manchester United won the first Premier League title with 84 points.
Coventry City survived relegation with 49. The gap: **35 points**.

In 2019/20, Liverpool won with 99 points. Aston Villa survived with 34.
The gap: **65 points**.

Is the Premier League becoming a two-tier league?

## Key Findings

::: {.callout-tip}
## Summary

- **Title-winning points have trended upward** at roughly +0.25 points per season
- **Relegation survival points are flat or declining** at roughly -0.15 points per season
- **The gap has nearly doubled**, from an average of 42 pts (1990s) to 58 pts (2020s)
- The sharpest inflection was **2016/17**, when Guardiola and Klopp triggered a points arms race
- **The same 6 clubs dominate the top 4** with increasing regularity
:::

```{python}
#| label: load-data-prem
#| code-summary: "Import utilities and load data"

import sys
sys.path.insert(0, "..")

from _utils import (
   LEAGUES, scrape_league, enrich_dataframe,
   build_league_chart, build_frequency_tables,
)
import pandas as pd
import numpy as np
import io
import html5lib

config = LEAGUES["premier_league"]
df = scrape_league("premier_league", cache_dir=".")
df = enrich_dataframe(df)

print(f"Dataset: {len(df)} seasons loaded")
df[["Season", "Champion", "Title-Winning Points", "4th Place", "4th Place Points",
   "Survived Relegation", "Relegation Survival Points",
   "Relegated 1", "Relegated 2", "Relegated 3"]].tail(10)
```

## Interactive Analysis

Use the **dropdown** to explore different eras. Each era recalculates its own
trend line. Hover over any point to see the champion, top 4, or relegated clubs.

```{python}
#| label: fig-main-prem
#| fig-cap: "Premier League points analysis with per-era trend lines"
#| code-summary: "Build main visualization"

eras = [
   {"label": "Full History (1992-2025)", "start": df["Season"].iloc[0],
    "end": df["Season"].iloc[-1],
    "title": "Premier League: The Growing Divide (1992-2025)"},
   {"label": "Pre-Abramovich (1992-2003)", "start": "1992/93", "end": "2002/03",
    "title": "Premier League: Pre-Abramovich Era (1992-2003)"},
   {"label": "Oligarch Era (2003-2016)", "start": "2003/04", "end": "2015/16",
    "title": "Premier League: The Oligarch Era (2003-2016)"},
   {"label": "Pep/Klopp Era (2016-2025)", "start": "2016/17",
    "end": df["Season"].iloc[-1],
    "title": "Premier League: The Pep/Klopp Era (2016-2025)"},
]

outliers = [
   {"season": "2003/04", "y": 90, "text": "Invincibles",
    "color": "#FFD54F", "ax": -50, "ay": -30},
   {"season": "2015/16", "y": 81, "text": "Leicester!",
    "color": "#66FF66", "ax": -50, "ay": -30},
   {"season": "2017/18", "y": 100, "text": "100 pts",
    "color": "#4FC3F7", "ax": 0, "ay": -25},
   {"season": "2019/20", "y": 99, "text": "COVID",
    "color": "#EF5350", "ax": 40, "ay": -20},
]

fig = build_league_chart(df, config, eras=eras, outliers=outliers)
fig.show()
```

## Champions League Concentration

```{python}
#| label: tbl-top4
#| tbl-cap: "Most frequent Premier League Top 4 finishers (1992-2025)"
#| code-summary: "Top 4 frequency"

top4_clubs, relegated_clubs = build_frequency_tables(df, config)
top4_clubs.head(10).style.format({"Percentage": "{:.1f}%"}).hide(axis="index")
```

## Relegated Clubs

```{python}
#| label: tbl-relegated
#| tbl-cap: "Most frequently relegated Premier League clubs"
#| code-summary: "Relegation frequency"

relegated_clubs.head(10).style.hide(axis="index")
```

## Era Comparison

```{python}
#| label: tbl-eras
#| tbl-cap: "Points statistics by era"
#| code-summary: "Era comparison table"

era_stats = []
for era in eras:
   start_idx = df[df["Season"] == era["start"]].index[0]
   end_idx = df[df["Season"] == era["end"]].index[0]
   era_slice = df.iloc[start_idx:end_idx + 1]
   era_stats.append({
       "Era": era["label"].split("(")[0].strip(),
       "Seasons": len(era_slice),
       "Avg Title Pts": round(era_slice["Title-Winning Points"].mean(), 1),
       "Avg Survival Pts": round(era_slice["Relegation Survival Points"].mean(), 1),
       "Avg Gap": round(era_slice["Gap"].mean(), 1),
       "Avg Ratio": round(era_slice["Ratio"].mean(), 2),
   })

pd.DataFrame(era_stats).style.format({"Avg Ratio": "{:.2f}x"}).hide(axis="index")
```

## Methodology

::: {.callout-note collapse="true"}
## Technical details

Data scraped from Wikipedia's individual season articles. The first three
seasons (1992/93 through 1994/95) had 22 teams; relegation positions are
adjusted accordingly. Trend lines use per-era linear regression via
`numpy.polyfit`. Caching avoids repeated Wikipedia requests.
:::

---

*Part of [The European Football Divide](../) project.*
```

---

## `projects/european-football-divide/bundesliga/index.qmd`

```markdown
---
title: "Bundesliga: Bayern's Iron Grip"
subtitle: "32 seasons of German football's competitive imbalance"
author: "Eric Schnitger"
date: 2026-04-27
categories: [data-visualization, python, sports-analytics, web-scraping]
image: thumbnail.png
description: |
 An interactive analysis of every Bundesliga season from 1992 to 2025,
 examining Bayern Munich's structural dominance and its effect on
 competitive balance.
format:
 html:
   code-fold: true
   code-summary: "Show code"
   code-tools: true
   toc: true
   toc-depth: 3
execute:
 echo: true
 warning: false
 freeze: auto
---

## The Question

Bayern Munich have won the Bundesliga more than any other club in history. But
has their dominance grown, or has it always been this extreme? And with the
50+1 rule supposedly ensuring competitive balance, why does Germany's league
look so predictable?

## Key Findings

::: {.callout-tip}
## Summary

- **Bayern have won ~21 of 32 titles** (66%) since 1992
- **The gap trend is flatter than the Premier League** because Bayern's dominance was already established
- **Dortmund's emergence** (2010-12) represents the only sustained challenge
- **The 18-team, 34-match format** produces lower raw point totals, but the ratio of champion to survival is comparable to other leagues
- **16th-place playoff** makes the relegation boundary more ambiguous than in other leagues
:::

```{python}
#| label: load-data
#| code-summary: "Import and load data"

import sys
sys.path.insert(0, "..")

from _utils import (
   LEAGUES, scrape_league, enrich_dataframe,
   build_league_chart, build_frequency_tables,
)
import pandas as pd
import numpy as np

config = LEAGUES["bundesliga"]
df = scrape_league("bundesliga", cache_dir=".")
df = enrich_dataframe(df)

print(f"Dataset: {len(df)} seasons loaded")
df[["Season", "Champion", "Title-Winning Points", "Survived Relegation",
   "Relegation Survival Points", "Relegated 1", "Relegated 2", "Relegated 3"]].tail(10)
```

## Interactive Analysis

```{python}
#| label: fig-main
#| fig-cap: "Bundesliga: Title-winning vs. relegation survival points"
#| code-summary: "Build visualization"

eras = [
   {"label": "Full History (1992-2025)", "start": df["Season"].iloc[0],
    "end": df["Season"].iloc[-1],
    "title": "Bundesliga: Title vs. Relegation (1992-2025)"},
   {"label": "Pre-Guardiola (1992-2013)", "start": "1992/93", "end": "2012/13",
    "title": "Bundesliga: Traditional Era (1992-2013)"},
   {"label": "Modern Bayern (2013-2025)", "start": "2013/14",
    "end": df["Season"].iloc[-1],
    "title": "Bundesliga: Modern Bayern (2013-2025)"},
]

outliers = [
   {"season": "2012/13", "y": 91, "text": "Bayern Treble",
    "color": "#EF5350", "ax": 0, "ay": -25},
]

# Fix y-values from actual data
for outlier in outliers:
   match = df[df["Season"] == outlier["season"]]
   if not match.empty and outlier["y"] is None:
       outlier["y"] = int(match["Title-Winning Points"].iloc[0])

fig = build_league_chart(df, config, eras=eras, outliers=outliers)
fig.show()
```

## Champions League Concentration

```{python}
#| label: tbl-top4
#| tbl-cap: "Most frequent Bundesliga Top 4 finishers"
#| code-summary: "Top 4 frequency"

top4_clubs, relegated_clubs = build_frequency_tables(df, config)
top4_clubs.head(10).style.format({"Percentage": "{:.1f}%"}).hide(axis="index")
```

## Relegated Clubs

```{python}
#| label: tbl-relegated
#| tbl-cap: "Most frequently relegated Bundesliga clubs"
#| code-summary: "Relegation frequency"

relegated_clubs.head(10).style.hide(axis="index")
```

## Context

- **18 teams**, 34 matches per season (lower point totals than 20-team leagues)
- **2 direct relegation + 1 playoff** (16th place plays 3rd in 2. Bundesliga)
- **50+1 ownership rule** prevents majority private ownership, yet Bayern's commercial dominance achieves a similar competitive imbalance through wages
- **Dortmund** (2011, 2012) and **Wolfsburg** (2009) are rare interlopers

---

*Part of [The European Football Divide](../) project.*
```
